Advances in SMR Developments 2024
download_details.error: trafilatura returned empty text (page may be JS-rendered or paywalled)

findings: A single SMR unit can cost up to US$1 billion, and combined or multi-modal configurations drive costs even higher, representing a significant capital barrier.
New nuclear energy designs often take up to 4 years for regulatory certification, and SMR deployment additionally requires site permitting and environmental impact assessments.

Many SMR vendors are pursuing novel, largely unvetted technologies—including molten salt, liquid metal cooling, and Light Water Reactor variants—creating technological uncertainty for investors and regulators.
Public safety concerns and fears reinforced by Chernobyl (1986) and Fukushima-Daiichi (2011) persist, and SMR deployment typically requires local community buy-in.
NuScale's valuation dropped 14.7% and Oklo's dropped 16.2% in the week of October 20–24, 2025, driven by concerns about project delays and perceived overvaluations.
